Name: rate_distortion_optimal_bit_allocation
Title: Optimal Bit Allocation for Model Compression via Rate-Distortion Theory
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that an optimal bit allocation framework based on rate-distortion theory can provide a tighter bound on the bits-per-weight needed for predictive sufficiency in neural networks, outperforming existing heuristic methods.
Related Work: While existing works, such as those by Gao et al. (2018) and Zuo et al. (2026), establish foundational limits for compression and propose practical implementations, they primarily focus on empirical evaluations without stringent theoretical guarantees. Our approach distinctly emphasizes a principled framework that connects rate-distortion bounds with quantization strategies, offering rigorous quantification of achievable rates and distortion under various conditions.
Abstract: This proposal aims to investigate the optimal bit allocation for model compression through a rigorous application of rate-distortion theory. We will derive a novel framework that quantifies the relationship between bits-per-weight, predictive sufficiency, and task-specific distortion. By establishing a theoretical foundation that outlines achievable rates for various neural architectures, we will develop a quantization strategy that dynamically adapts to task requirements while minimizing distortion. This work not only bridges theoretical insights with practical applications but also provides a reproducible pathway for achieving optimal model compression under defined information-theoretic constraints. We will empirically validate our framework through a series of experiments, demonstrating its superiority over existing heuristic methods in maintaining predictive performance.
Experiments: Develop a theoretical framework that derives achievable rates for specific neural architectures (e.g., feedforward networks, CNNs, LSTMs) under defined distortion measures.
Implement a quantization scheme based on the derived achievable rates, applying it to benchmark datasets across different models.
Conduct experiments to compare the performance of the proposed quantization method against existing heuristic methods in terms of accuracy and distortion metrics (e.g., mean squared error).
Utilize metrics such as bits-per-weight and task-relevant distortion to evaluate the efficiency of the proposed approach.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The theoretical derivations may be complex, and proving the achievability of bounds for non-linear models could be challenging.
The empirical results may vary based on the choice of datasets and model architectures, potentially limiting generalizability.
The adoption of a new quantization scheme may require extensive calibration, impacting practical deployment.
